137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.

207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
304	
305	```markdown

320	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
322	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
323	- **Fixed dependencies** `<label>` — corrected/added `\uses{...}` (was isolated / had a broken ref per leandag).
324	- **Removed** `<label>` — orphaned isolated block, removal authorized by directive.
325	- ...
326	
327	## Cross-references introduced
328	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
329	- ...

The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
333	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
334	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.
335

338

341	- ...

2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in

68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic

71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from

74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).

236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}

283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}

306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.

356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean. As of iter-020 the present theorem IS a closed, axiom-clean Lean
408	  %   declaration (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational); the ambient subquotient route
409	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
410	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
411	\end{lemma}

413	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
414	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
415	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
416	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
417	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
418	%  following short exact sequence
419	%  $$
420	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
421	%  $$
422	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
423	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	    \leanok
435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}

497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}

574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
600	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
617	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
621	[Closure under difference]
622	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
624	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
625	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
626	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
627	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
628	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
629	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
630	\end{lemma}
631	
632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
633	[Closure under right shift]
634	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
636	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
637	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
638	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
639	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
640	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
641	  Lean.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}\leanok
645	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
646	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
648	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
649	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
651	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
652	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
653	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
654	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
659	  \leanok
660	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
661	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
662	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
663	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
664	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
665	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
666	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
667	\end{proof}

669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
671	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
673	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
674	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
675	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
676	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
677	  \[
678	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
679	  \]
680	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
681	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
682	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
683	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
684	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
685	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
686	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
687	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
688	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
689	\end{lemma}
690	
691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
693	  \leanok
694	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
695	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
696	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
697	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
698	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
699	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
700	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
701	\end{proof}

715	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
716	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
717	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
718	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,

721	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
722	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
723	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
724	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
725	  \[
726	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
727	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
728	  \]
729	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
730	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.

733	\begin{proof}
734	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
735	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
736	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
737	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
738	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
739	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
740	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
741	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
742	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
743	  function
744	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
745	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
746	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
747	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
748	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
749	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
750	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
751	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
752	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
753	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
754	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
755	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
756	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
757	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
758	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
759	\end{proof}

2442	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the

2447	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2448	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2449	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2450	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2451	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2452	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2453	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a
quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
$\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to
the $R$-module $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
Since every standard open $D(f)$ is quasi-compact we see that
$X$ is locally quasi-compact, i.e., every point has a fundamental
system of quasi-compact neighbourhoods, see Topology,
Definition \ref{topology-definition-locally-quasi-compact}.
Hence by Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-quasi-coherent-module}
for every prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$ corresponding to $x \in X$
there exists an open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ such that
$\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$.
In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
covering to  a standard open covering.
Thus we get a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup D(f_i)$
and $R_{f_i}$-modules $M_i$ and isomorphisms
$\varphi_i : \mathcal{F}|_{D(f_i)} \to \mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
for some $R_{f_i}$-module $M_i$. On the overlaps
we get isomorphisms
$$
\xymatrix{
\mathcal{F}_{M_i}|_{D(f_if_j)}
\ar[rr]^{\varphi_i^{-1}|_{D(f_if_j)}}
& &
\mathcal{F}|_{D(f_if_j)}
\ar[rr]^{\varphi_j|_{D(f_if_j)}}
& &
\mathcal{F}_{M_j}|_{D(f_if_j)}.
}
$$
Let us denote these $\psi_{ij}$. It is clear that
we have the cocycle condition
$$
\psi_{jk}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
\circ
\psi_{ij}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
=
\psi_{ik}|_{D(f_if_jf_k)}
$$
on triple overlaps.

\medskip\noindent
Recall that each of the open subspaces $D(f_i)$, $D(f_if_j)$,
$D(f_if_jf_k)$ is an affine scheme. Hence the sheaves $\mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
are isomorphic to the sheaves $\widetilde M_i$ by Lemma
\ref{lemma-compare-constructions} above. In particular we see that
$\mathcal{F}_{M_i}(D(f_if_j)) = (M_i)_{f_j}$, etc.
Also by Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} above we see
that $\psi_{ij}$ corresponds to a unique $R_{f_if_j}$-module isomorphism
$$
\psi_{ij} : (M_i)_{f_j} \longrightarrow (M_j)_{f_i}
$$
namely, the effect of $\psi_{ij}$ on sections over $D(f_if_j)$.
Moreover these then satisfy the cocycle condition that
$$
\xymatrix{
(M_i)_{f_jf_k}
\ar[rd]_{\psi_{ij}}
\ar[rr]^{\psi_{ik}}
& &
(M_k)_{f_if_j} \\
&
(M_j)_{f_if_k} \ar[ru]_{\psi_{jk}}
}
$$
commutes (for any triple $i, j, k$).

\medskip\noindent
Now Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-glue-modules}
shows that there exist an $R$-module $M$ such that
$M_i = M_{f_i}$ compatible with the morphisms $\psi_{ij}$.
Consider $\mathcal{F}_M = \widetilde M$. At this point it is
a formality to show that $\widetilde M$ is isomorphic to
the quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we started out with.
Namely, the sheaves $\mathcal{F}$ and $\widetilde M$ give
rise to isomorphic sets of glueing data of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules

% NOTE: Archon-original Route-F realization -- the module analogue of Mathlib's structure-sheaf qcqs
% lemma `isLocalization_basicOpen_of_qcqs`: the IsLocalizedModule witness is assembled from its three
% pointwise fields by compact-open induction + sheaf gluing, with NO equalizer object and NO flat
% descent. The Stacks 01HA statement citation above is the source for the LEMMA STATEMENT; the proof
% skeleton is the qcqs template (not a transcribed Stacks proof), so no `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF`.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_of_qcqs_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_constructor_mathlib,
    lem:isUnit_res_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:compact_open_induction_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict,
    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  Write \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\). We build the witness directly as the three-field predicate
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_constructor_mathlib})
  on the restriction \(N \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\), mirroring exactly how the structure-sheaf qcqs lemma
  \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_of_qcqs_mathlib} assembles \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\)
  from its three pointwise axioms. No finite equalizer object and no flatness of localization are
  used.

  \emph{Shared core (step 1 -- the only manual ingredient also present in the equalizer route).}
  From the quasi-coherence datum \(M.\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\) extract a finite basic-open tilde
  cover: refine the given cover to the basis of basic opens, take a finite subcover by
  quasi-compactness of \(\Spec R\), and transport the local presentation across each affine
  identification \(D(g) \cong \Spec R_g\), using \(\Gamma(\Spec R, D(g)) = R_g\)
  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}). On each \(D(g)\) the transported presentation makes
  \(M|_{D(g)} \cong \widetilde{N_g}\) (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib}), so the
  affine tilde localization (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}) computes the sections of
  \(M\) on sub-basic-opens of \(D(g)\). Unlike the equalizer route, Route~F needs no explicit
  equalizer/limit object and no proof that the \(g_a\) generate the unit ideal.

  \emph{map\_units.} The \(R\)-action of \(f^n\) on \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) factors through
  multiplication by the restriction of \(f\) to \(D(f)\), which is a unit of the structure ring
  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}, D(f))\) (\cref{lem:isUnit_res_basicOpen_mathlib}); hence \(f^n\) acts
  invertibly on \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\).

  \emph{surj.} Every section of \(M\) on \(D(f)\) is \(f^{-n}\) times the restriction of a global
  section, for some \(n\). This is proved by induction over the compact open \(D(f)\)
  (\cref{lem:compact_open_induction_mathlib}): the base case is an affine \(D(g)\), where the claim
  is the affine tilde localization from step~1; the inductive step glues a section already defined
  on an open \(S\) with one on an affine \(D(g)\), agreeing on the overlap, via the sheaf
  Mayer--Vietoris square (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}) and unique gluing
  (\cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the concrete sheaf
  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M)\).

  \emph{exists\_of\_eq.} If a global section restricts to \(0\) on \(D(f)\), then \(f^n\) annihilates
  it for some \(n\). The same compact-open induction applies, with the base case again the affine
  tilde localization and the inductive step the uniqueness half of gluing
  (\(\mathrm{eq\_of\_locally\_eq'}\), \cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}).

  Supplying these three fields constructs \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the
  restriction \(N \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\), as required.
\end{proof}

% --- Quasi-coherence datum and the gap1 sub-build ---

\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherence = a cover with local presentations]
  \label{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
  \lean{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent}).}
  A sheaf of modules \(M\) on a scheme is \emph{quasi-coherent}
  (\(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,M\)) exactly when the type of \emph{quasi-coherence data}
  \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,M\) is nonempty. A datum
  \(q : \mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,M\) consists of an index family \(\{X_i\}\) of opens covering the
  whole scheme together with, for each \(i\), a presentation
  \((M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i).\mathrm{Presentation}\) of the restriction of \(M\) to \(X_i\)
  (a cokernel-of-free-modules description on the site over \(X_i\)). Thus quasi-coherence packages a
  cover on which \(M\) is locally presented; on each member of the cover
  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib} applies.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Finite basic-open cover trivializing the $\widetilde{(-)}$-counit (gap1 sub-build)]
  \label{lem:exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}

  % current QUOT prover target. It isolates the only manual content (producing local tilde
  % identifications on a FINITE basic-open cover) from the gap1 gluing assembly.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}, proof,
  % covering step (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1289-L1303). Cf. Stacks 01HA.
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "Hence by Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-quasi-coherent-module}
  %  for every prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$ corresponding to $x \in X$
  %  there exists an open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ such that
  %  $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
  %  sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$.
  %  In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
  %  By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
  %  covering to  a standard open covering.
  %  Thus we get a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup D(f_i)$
  %  and $R_{f_i}$-modules $M_i$ and isomorphisms
  %  $\varphi_i : \mathcal{F}|_{D(f_i)} \to \mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
  %  for some $R_{f_i}$-module $M_i$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}, covering
  step of the proof; cf.\ Stacks 01HA.}
  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\). Then there is a \emph{finite} family
  of basic opens \(\{D(g_j)\}_{j}\) with \(\bigsqcup_j D(g_j) = \top\) (their join is the whole
  space) such that for each \(j\) the restriction of \(M\) to \(D(g_j)\) has trivial

  Equivalently, on each \(D(g_j)\) the sheaf \(M\) is identified with \(\widetilde{N_j}\) for a module
  \(N_j\) over \(R_{g_j} = \Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(g_j))\).

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  From the quasi-coherence datum (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}) take the
  cover \(\{X_i\}\) of \(\top\) with local presentations \((M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i).\mathrm{Presentation}\).

  \(\Spec R\) is quasi-compact (\(\mathrm{CompactSpace}\,(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum}\,R)\)), finitely many
  of these basic opens \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_m)\) already cover \(\top\). On each \(D(g_j)\) the local
  presentation transports across the affine identification \(D(g_j) \cong \Spec R_{g_j}\) (the
  site-\(\mathrm{over}\) presentation maps to a presentation of the restricted sheaf under the
  restriction functor, via \(\mathrm{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) / \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\)),
  yielding a global presentation of \(M|_{D(g_j)}\) over \(R_{g_j}\). A global presentation forces the
  counit to be an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib}), so
  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl((M|_{D(g_j)}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\) and \(M|_{D(g_j)} \cong
  \widetilde{N_j}\) with \(N_j = \Gamma(M, D(g_j))\); the per-\(D(g_j)\) base identification of
  sections is then \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict} together with
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent on an affine has trivial $\widetilde{(-)}$-counit (gap1)]
  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}

  % bridge for this (no global-presentation-from-quasicoherent, no IsQuasicoherent → tilde.essImage);
  % the stalk/local-presentation shortcuts do not avoid it.
  % Route: analogies/quot-qcoh-affine-globalization.md.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}
  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1279--1284).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
  % be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then
  % $\mathcal{F}$ is isomorphic to the sheaf associated to the $R$-module $\Gamma(X,
  % \mathcal{F})$."
  \textit{The affine equivalence QCoh\((\Spec R)\) \(\simeq\) Mod\((R)\): a quasi-coherent module
  sheaf on \(\Spec R\) is \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M)}\) up to canonical isomorphism.}
  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) that is quasi-coherent
  (\(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,M\)). Then the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism;
  equivalently \(M\) lies in the essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}(R) \to (\Spec
  R).\mathrm{Modules}\).
\end{lemma}
% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma lemma-quasi-coherent-affine, proof, gluing
% step (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1354-L1360).
% "Now Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-glue-modules}
%  shows that there exist an $R$-module $M$ such that
%  $M_i = M_{f_i}$ compatible with the morphisms $\psi_{ij}$.
%  Consider $\mathcal{F}_M = \widetilde M$. At this point it is
%  a formality to show that $\widetilde M$ is isomorphic to
%  the quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we started out with."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  By the sub-build \cref{lem:exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover} there is a finite basic-open
  cover \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_m)\) of \(\top\) on each of which the restricted counit is an
  isomorphism, so \(M|_{D(g_j)} \cong \widetilde{N_j}\) with \(N_j = \Gamma(M, D(g_j))\) a module over
  \(R_{g_j}\). On the overlaps \(D(g_jg_k)\) these identifications differ by canonical localization
  isomorphisms \((N_j)_{g_k} \cong (N_k)_{g_j}\) (the sections being computed by
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}), satisfying the cocycle condition on triple overlaps.
  Gluing this descent datum produces an \(R\)-module \(N\) with \(N_{g_j} \cong N_j\) and a comparison
  map \(\widetilde{N} \to M\); concretely the gluing is carried out at the level of the underlying
  sheaf \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M)\) by the sheaf Mayer--Vietoris square
  (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}) and unique gluing of compatible sections
  (\cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}, with its companion \(\mathrm{eq\_of\_locally\_eq'}\) for
  uniqueness) -- a module port of \(\mathrm{exists\_eq\_pow\_mul\_of\_isCompact\_of\_isQuasiSeparated}\)
  that sidesteps the \(\mathrm{CommRingCat}\)-specific \(\mathrm{objSupIsoProdEqLocus}\). Because the
  comparison restricts to an isomorphism on each member of a cover, it is an isomorphism of sheaves;
  hence the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism, identifying \(M\) with
  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)}\).
\end{proof}
